retrieved_docs.title: Hepatitis C -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: The diagnosis of HCV infection is based principally on the detection of antibodies to recombinant HCV polypeptides and by assays for HCV RNA. These enzyme immunoassays measure antibodies directed against NS4, core, NS3, and NS5 sequences. These cannot differentiate between past or current HCV infection. Direct testing for HCV RNA is necessary to distinguish between ongoing or prior infection in persons with HCV antibodies. HCV Rapid Antibody Test with rapid turnover can be an essential public health tool in nontraditional settings. There are 3 scenarios in which the HCV RNA test should be considered upfront: Exposure within the past 6 months An immunocompromised host Suspicion for reinfection

retrieved_docs.title: Viral Hepatitis -- Evaluation -- Hepatitis C
retrieved_docs.content: In the setting of a positive hepatitis C antibody test, HCV RNA testing is necessary to confirm infection. It is the most specific test for HCV infection and can detect acute infection even before antibodies develop. It is also helpful in discriminating between true infection and false-positive cases, indeterminate results, and rare cases where perinatal transmission may have taken place. (see Table. Hepatitis C Stages and Serological Markers). HCV RNA testing also provides information on viral load and HCV genotype, which can help tailor treatment and predict response to therapy. In recent years, testing for HCV genotype has become less important as some of the available treatment options are pan-genotypic (able to effectively treat infection regardless of the genotype). Knowing the genotype may be more relevant in previously failed treatment patients. During the initial evaluation, patients should be asked about prior treatment as this may play a role in medical regimen selection, and testing for viral resistance may be indicated.

retrieved_docs.title: Viral Hepatitis -- Evaluation -- Hepatitis C
retrieved_docs.content: HCV exposure can be detected with antibodies to the HCV (anti-HCV). These assays can detect antibodies within 4 to 10 weeks of infection. However, anti-HCV can remain negative for a few months after an acute HCV infection, although once it appears in the blood, it generally remains present for life. Unlike anti-HBs, anti-HCV is not a protective antibody and does not offer immunity with subsequent exposure to HCV.

retrieved_docs.title: Vertical Transplacental Infections -- Evaluation -- Hepatitis C Virus
retrieved_docs.content: Infant HCV evaluation should include an HCV antibody test at approximately 18 months of age or an HCV RNA polymerase chain reaction (PCR) test between 1 and 2 months of age. [65] If HCV infection is suspected, serial monitoring of liver function with transaminases and coagulation studies is reasonable.

retrieved_docs.title: Minimal Change Disease -- Evaluation -- Labs to rule out secondary causes:
retrieved_docs.content: Antinuclear antibodies Hepatitis B and C serologic tests Serologic test for syphilis (e.g., rapid plasma reagin) HIV antibody test Complement levels (CH50, C3, C4)

retrieved_docs.title: Viral Hepatitis -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: In addition to these initial routine laboratory tests, other specific tests are available to evaluate for the type of viral hepatitis as follows:

retrieved_docs.title: Viral Hepatitis -- Evaluation -- Hepatitis A
retrieved_docs.content: The standard test for diagnosing acute infection with HAV is the presence of immunoglobulin M (IgM) antibodies to HAV. IgM antibody disappears a few months after the acute infection. Immunoglobulin G (IgG) antibody to HAV is detected in the later stages of infection. The presence of IgG antibodies to the HAV means that the patient has been infected with the HAV in the past, as recently as approximately 2 months to as long ago as several decades. Therefore, IgG antibodies to HAV do not indicate acute infection and should not be used to screen for such. Most patients with IgG antibodies to HAV have lifelong immunity against the HAV infection [23] .

retrieved_docs.title: Viral Hepatitis -- Evaluation -- Hepatitis C
retrieved_docs.content: Liver enzyme testing can indicate the presence of liver injury but is not very accurate in predicting the severity of the disease. Liver function testing can be more valuable in monitoring therapeutic response to HCV treatment, with improvement in aminotransferase levels indicating patients responding to the treatment while worsening of aminotransferase levels post HCV treatment may indicate a relapse. However, it is more common to use HCV RNA as the test of choice to monitor the response to treatment. [39] [40]

retrieved_docs.title: Cryoglobulinemic Vasculitis(Archived) -- Evaluation -- Infection Workup
retrieved_docs.content: Viral hepatitis screening, including anti-hepatitis C viral antibody, hepatitis C viral RNA PCR, hepatitis B surface antigen, hepatitis B surface antibody, and hepatitis B core antibody, shall be performed to detect underlying viral hepatitis. Workup for other potential infectious etiology can be considered on an individual basis.

retrieved_docs.title: Transfusion Transmitted Disease -- Evaluation -- Viral Testing
retrieved_docs.content: Donation centers screen for HCV using tests for anti-HCV antibodies and NAT detection of HCV RNA. In the US, the standard of care for detecting HCV infection involves mini-pool NAT (MP NAT). This method tests small aliquots from donations of 4 to 16 donors as a single sample. [35] The current method's window period is 1 to 2 weeks. MP NAT also detects HIV RNA and has replaced p24 antigen testing of donor blood. The window period for HIV using MP NAT is approximately 11 days.

retrieved_docs.title: Viral Hepatidities (Archived) -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: HCV: Acute infection is indicated by the presence of HCV RNA with or without the presence of IgM antibody. Chronic infection is indicated by the presence of HCA RNA with the presence of the IgG antibody. If a patient clears the infection, results will show no detectable HCV RNA, with or without the presence of HCV antibodies. HDV: Antibodies indicate exposure to the virus while the viral load is used to detect current infection.

retrieved_docs.title: HIV Prophylaxis (Archived) -- Issues of Concern -- It recommends HIV testing of the source patient whenever possible. Voluntary testing of the source patient is required, and laws on consent for testing differ by state. A rapid test is mandatory where available. If the source patient reports a high-risk exposure within the preceding six weeks, some guidelines (though not the CDC) recommend HIV RNA assay. HIV RNA can test the patient for infection during the "window period," which is the time frame between contracting the virus and developing an antibody response. However, there have been no documented cases of transmission during this window period. The source patient may test for hepatitis C (HCV) RNA and hepatitis B (HBV) surface antigen.
retrieved_docs.content: Baseline HIV testing of the exposed worker should be done after occupational exposure, even if the exposed worker declines PEP. It can then perform repeat HIV testing at 4 weeks, 12 weeks, and 6 months post-exposure. Other baseline tests conducted on the patient at the time of the initial presentation should include HCV antibody and HBV if the individual has not been vaccinated against hepatitis B. Recommended Treatment It recommends a 28-day course of a 3-drug regimen for HIV-negative patients who need oPEP. The recommended regimen includes tenofovir DF 300 mg and a fixed-dose combination of emtricitabine 200 mg once daily with raltegravir 400 mg twice daily. Wound treatment: The wound from a needle stick should be washed with soap and running water. Splashes to the eyes, mouth, or skin should be irrigated with water or saline. Avoid using caustic agents like bleach. There is no evidence that using antiseptics reduces the risk of HIV transmission. Follow-up

retrieved_docs.title: Viral Hepatitis -- Deterrence and Patient Education -- Hepatitis C
retrieved_docs.content: Vaccines against HCV are currently unavailable, and immunoglobulin has not been proven effective in preventing transmission. It is, therefore, of high importance to put infection control practices in place to prevent the contamination of blood, organs, and semen and prevent the transmission of the virus into donor pools with better screening and improved viral elimination techniques. [60] Pregnant mothers testing positive for HCV should be offered treatment after delivery. Infants born to mothers with HCV infection should have testing done at 2 months and then at 18 months to detect infection.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 2620
retrieved_docs.content: Alcoholic hepatitis (تَشَمَّعٌ كُحولِيّ) is a liver disorder caused by excessive alcohol consumption that can progress to hepatic cirrhosis (تَشَمُّع كَبِدي). This relationship highlights the potential evolution from an inflammatory, potentially reversible condition to an irreversible stage of extensive liver scarring, emphasizing the importance of early detection and treatment of alcoholic hepatitis to prevent the development of cirrhosis.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 2 -- Community 483
retrieved_docs.content: Antivirals for treatment of HCV infections are direct acting antivirals, indicating that these medications specifically target viral proteins essential for hepatitis C virus replication.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 285
retrieved_docs.content: Ribavirin is classified as a member of the group of antivirals used for the treatment of hepatitis C virus (HCV) infections, indicating its role within this therapeutic class. In addition to its use against HCV, ribavirin may also help prevent viral infections (عدوى فيروسية). Remdesivir, another antiviral agent, may be employed to treat viral infections (عدوى فيروسية). Together, ribavirin and remdesivir exemplify antiviral medications that are utilized both for treating specific viral diseases such as HCV and for broader management of viral infections, underscoring their importance in antiviral therapy and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 2 -- Community 655
retrieved_docs.content: Antivirals for treatment of HCV infections include ribavirin and telaprevir, both of which are employed as therapeutic agents against hepatitis C virus. Remdesivir has been identified as a medication that may treat عدوى فيروسية (viral infection), while ribavirin is also noted for its potential to prevent عدوى فيروسية. Together, these antiviral drugs illustrate the spectrum of strategies used to both treat and prevent viral diseases, underscoring their importance in clinical management of infections such as hepatitis C and other viral pathogens.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 2 -- Community 369
retrieved_docs.content: Ribavirin is an antiviral medication that belongs to the class of antivirals used for the treatment of hepatitis C virus (HCV) infections, making it a member of the therapeutic group “Antivirals for treatment of HCV infections.”

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 638
retrieved_docs.content: Antivirals for treatment of HCV infections are classified as direct‑acting antivirals, and telaprevir is a member of this antiviral group used specifically to treat hepatitis C virus infection.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 262
retrieved_docs.content: The therapeutic category “Antivirals for treatment of HCV infections” comprises agents such as ribavirin and telaprevir, both of which are employed as antiviral medications specifically to manage hepatitis C virus (HCV) infection.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 1856
retrieved_docs.content: الاعتماد على الكحول (alcohol dependence) is clinically associated with اِلْتِهَابُ الكَبِد (liver inflammation), indicating that chronic alcohol use can contribute to inflammatory damage of the liver. Hepatitis is classified as اِلْتِهَابُ الكَبِد, meaning that hepatitis represents a specific type of liver inflammation often caused by viral infection, autoimmune processes, or toxic insults. Together, these relationships highlight that liver inflammation can arise both from alcohol dependence and from hepatitis, underscoring the importance of evaluating alcohol use and identifying hepatitis when assessing patients with hepatic inflammatory conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 627
retrieved_docs.content: Remdesivir may be used to treat عدوى فيروسية, offering therapeutic benefit for patients with viral infections, whereas ribavirin may be employed to prevent عدوى فيروسية, serving as a prophylactic antiviral option. Both agents are relevant in the clinical management of viral diseases, with remdesivir focusing on treatment and ribavirin on prevention.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 2074
retrieved_docs.content: Hepatitis, which is classified as اِلْتِهَابُ الكَبِد (liver inflammation), is clinically associated with متلازمة الامتناع عن تعاطي الكحول (alcohol withdrawal syndrome). This relationship indicates that patients experiencing alcohol abstinence may also develop or have concurrent liver inflammation, underscoring the importance of monitoring hepatic function and providing appropriate liver‑focused care during the management of alcohol withdrawal.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2341
retrieved_docs.content: Failure (biologic function) is associated with الفَشَلٌ الكَبِدِيُّ الكُلْوِيّ, indicating that a general loss of biological function can be linked to hepatorenal failure—a severe condition in which both liver and kidney functions are compromised. Recognizing this association is important for clinicians, as it highlights the need to assess and manage systemic organ failure comprehensively in affected patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2274
retrieved_docs.content: Alcoholic cirrhosis (تَشَمَّعٌ كُحولِيّ) is a specific subtype of liver cirrhosis (تَشَمُّع كَبِدي). Liver cirrhosis, in turn, is clinically associated with esophageal variceal bleeding (نزيف دَوالِي المريء). Recognizing that patients with alcoholic cirrhosis develop the broader condition of liver cirrhosis helps clinicians anticipate the high risk of variceal hemorrhage, prompting appropriate screening, monitoring, and preventive interventions to improve patient outcomes.
